title: Meta-Learning: Why It’s Hard and What We Can Do
speaker: Ke Li
abstract: Meta-learning (or learning to learn) studies how to use machine learning to design machine learning methods themselves. We consider an optimization-based formulation of meta-learning that learns to design an optimization algorithm automatically, which we call Learning to Optimize. Surprisingly, it turns out that the most straightforward approach of learning such an algorithm, namely backpropagation, does not work.

proof_stages.label: Unified taxonomy of meta-learning formulations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines meta-learning as using machine learning to design machine-learning methods, distinguishes meta- and base-learning, and compares multitask learning, hyperparameter optimization, automatic model selection, and program induction by their search spaces and parameters.

proof_stages.label: Design criteria for meta-learning problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk extracts general design axes: what counts as a base-learning instance, what commonality exists across instances, whether meta-parameters can generalize, how large and parameterized the search space is, and whether it can be searched efficiently.

proof_stages.label: Optimization-based meta-learning as learning an optimizer
proof_stages.summary: Using the premise that much of machine learning reduces to optimization, the speaker formulates a learned optimizer as a parametric update rule that maps gradients, histories, iterates, and objective values to parameter updates, with objective functions as training/test instances and cumulative optimization loss as the meta-loss.

proof_stages.label: Naive unrolled backpropagation and overfitting failure
proof_stages.summary: The straightforward approach of differentiating through the unrolled optimization trajectory works on training objectives but can diverge on unseen objectives because a learned optimizer can memorize training optima and identify a finite training objective in logarithmically many queries, while simple fixes such as more objectives or short Markov histories are inadequate.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic Markov forward-dynamics formulation
proof_stages.summary: To reduce the train/test mismatch, the deterministic objective-specific forward dynamics are replaced during meta-training by stochastic Markov dynamics obtained by marginalizing over possible training objectives, so the optimizer trains under uncertainty similar to test time while the optimizer itself may still use long histories.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to reinforcement learning
proof_stages.summary: Because the stochastic meta-objective contains an intractable expectation over exponentially many trajectories, the problem is mapped to reinforcement learning with iterates as states, updates as actions, the learned update rule as policy, and objective values as costs.

proof_stages.label: Empirical behavior and transfer of learned optimizers
proof_stages.summary: Experiments with a coordinate-wise LSTM optimizer trained on an MNIST neural-network objective and tested on unseen objectives show adaptive large steps, recovery from overshoot or noisy gradients, and stability over longer horizons, with comparisons to backprop-trained learned optimizers and classical optimizers.

proof_stages.label: Open challenges in reliability and scaling
proof_stages.summary: The speaker concludes that the method is limited by the high-variance, intractable reinforcement-learning objective and by the quadratic space needed to model general forward dynamics, leaving open how to scale beyond coordinate-wise assumptions while allowing interactions and variable dimension.
